steps.title: Use the scatterplot trend
steps.content: The scatterplot decreases as $x$ increases, so the model needs a negative slope. This eliminates the other choices immediately because their slopes are positive.

steps.title: Estimate intercept and slope
steps.content: The left side of the scatterplot is near $y=10$ when $x$ is near $0$, so the $y$-intercept should be around $9$ or $10$. The right side is near $y=1$ or $2$ when $x$ is near $9$, so the slope is about $$\frac{2-10}{9-0}\approx -0.9.$$

steps.title: Compare the remaining claims
steps.content: Compare the two negative-slope choices. A choice with intercept $0.9$ and slope $-9.4$, so it would start near $y=1$ and drop almost $10$ units for every $1$ unit increase in $x$. That is much too steep for the scatterplot. The matching choice has intercept $9.4$ and slope $-0.9$, matching the visible starting value and gradual decrease.

steps.title: Confirm the choice
steps.content: A choice with a negative slope, but its intercept is only $0.9$ and its slope $-9.4$ is much too steep. The matching choice is the only line with both the right intercept and the right negative slope, so D is correct.
